metadata.title: SYNGO_BP_358
metadata.comment:
Rationale: This publication provides multiple lines of evidence that DSyd1 is involved the formation of functional synaptic terminals at the fly neuromuscular junction.

-Deletion of DSyd1 leads to a smaller NMJ, and a reduced number of active zones per NMJ (Figure 4 E-G).
-DSyd1 deletion gives rise to improper localization of active zone proteins BRP (Figure 5) and DLiprin-a (Figure 10)
-Electron microscopy reveals an abnormal organization of the T-bar, a critical morphological component of active zones at the NMJ (Figure 6).
-In addition to presynaptic defects, DSyd1 deletion leads to defects of the postsynapse, including a reduction of PSDs per NMJ (Figure 4 H and the size of GluR clusters (Figure 7)
-DSyd1 deletion results in a reduction of action-potential induced synaptic transmission (figure 4).
-In line with a role in the formation of functional active zones, DSyd1 arrives at nascent synaptic terminals before the active zone marker BRP (Figure 9).

In mammalian synapses, the mSyd1a has been suggested to play a role in active zone organization, but current available data are inconclusive, and homology between mSyd1a and DSyd1 is limited (Wentzel et.al. 2013).. Experimental description: Genetics. Fly strains were reared under standard laboratory conditions (Sigrist et al.,
2003).
